details.text:
وَالتِّينِ وَالزَّيْتُونِ

- “(I swear) by التِّينِ (the Teen - fig) and الزَّيْتُونِ (the Zaytun olive)” – There are various opinions regarding the intended meaning of these two fruits. Two opinions which were held in the earliest generations was that firstly they refer to the fruits that are eaten and secondly that they refer to places related to the fruits.

Derivative: سِينِينَ is commonly understood to mean Sinai and طُورِ سِينِينَ is Mount Sinai where Allah spoke to Musa (as). In Surah Al-Mu’mineen Allah says: طُورِ سَيْنَاءَ to refer to Mount Sinai and it is said that both words سِينِينَ (Sineen) and سَيْنَاءَ (Saynaa’) were known in the language of the Jews to refer to Sinai.

Derivative: طُورِ (Tur) is considered by some not to be an Arabic word in origin whilst others have stated that it is or came to be part of the Arabic language. It has been said to refer to a mountain that is lush and fall of trees. طُورِ (Tur) is commonly understood to refer to a mountain.
Derivative: الْبَلَدِ means a city which has defined borders. It refers to Makkah here and by using the word بَلَد it implies that Makkah is an established city.

Derivative: الْأَمِينِ is a description which can come from أَمانَة which refers to a trust and can also come from أَمْن which refers to peace, safety and security. All these meanings can be intended by the description of الْأَمِينِ.

Derivative: تَقْوِيمٍ from قَوَّمَ means to make something stand up straight, like if something is bent and then it is straightened and made right. It implies balancing multiple components together until they are perfectly straight, just as it means to design something perfectly for its purpose. تَقْوِيمٍ can also therefore refer to fashioning, shaping or form.
Derivative: أَحْسَنِ comes from حُسْن (Husn) which means beauty. It refers to beauty that is both on the outside and inside, literal and figurative. أَحْسَنِ means the best or most beautiful.
Derivative: لَقَدْ is made up of لَ and قَد and is used with the past tense (الماضِي – Al-Maadiy) for the purpose of emphasis and implying certainty.

لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنسَانَ فِي أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ

- This is the جَوابُ القَسْم (answer to the oath) for the preceding oaths.
- خَلَقْنَا “We created” – the “we” form is used to demonstrate power and control in addition to closeness to the act and object or result of the act.
Derivative: رَدَدْنَا is from رّدَّ which means to return or reject.
Derivative: سَافِلِينَ is the plural of سَافِل (Saafil) which means low from the perspective of status and dignity and refers to being low and inferior.
Derivative: ثُمَّ means “then” but implies a gap in time between what was before and what was after.

ثُمَّ رَدَدْنَاهُ أَسْفَلَ سَافِلِينَ

- “Then (ثُمَّ) We returned him to being (رَدَدْنَاهُ) the lowest (أَسْفَلَ) of the low (سَافِلِينَ)”. There are various interpretations for this verse. These include that it is referring to the human being after being created in the best of forms is returned to a frail and decrepit state when he gets old. Another interpretation is that after being created in the best of forms he is returned to being the lowest of the low in hellfire due to his disbelief and disobedience to Allah. A third interpretation is that the human deviates from his natural Fitrah (nature) through his disbelief and sinful conduct to become the lowest of the low.

Derivative: مَمْنُونٍ comes from the word مَنَّ which can mean to give a favour or to cut something. Here, in relation to أَجْر (Ajr -reward and recompense for effort) it means cut i.e., for them is an أَجْر (Ajr – reward) that is غَيْر (not) مَمْنُونٍ (cut off). Not being cut off means uninterrupted or perpetual. According to the other meaning of مَنَّ which is to be given a favour, أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُون would imply that it will be a reward that is given without asking for anything in return i.e., the reward will not be based on the premise of being given a favour.

فَمَا يُكَذِّبُكَ بَعْدُ بِالدِّينِ

- Depending on the meaning of مَا here, the verse can have two meanings. If the مَا is ما الاِسْتِفْهام (The Maa of questioning) with the meaning of “what”, then the intended meaning of فَمَا يُكَذِّبُكَ بَعْدُ بِالدِّينِ would be: “Then, what still makes, brings you or causes you to lie against or deny Ad-Deen i.e., the Day of recompense”. However, if the ما is مَا المَصْدَرِيَة with the meaning of “Who” (مَنْ), then the meaning would be: “Then, who would still lie against or deny you (referring to the Messenger ﷺ) in respect to Ad-Deen i.e., what he brought to be followed”.

Derivative: Both أَحْكَمِ and حاكِم come from the word حَكْمَة (Hikmah) which means wisdom or the word حُكْم (Hukm) which means judgement. The superlative form أَحْكَمِ (Ahkam) can therefore mean “the wisest” or “the best or most precise in judgement”. حَاكِم can mean a wiseman and also a ruler who passes judgements. The meanings can also be combined to provide four possibilities: 1) “The wisest of the wise”, 2) “The best judge among the wise”, 3) “The best judge amongst all judges” and 4) “The wisest of all rulers or judges”.
